SOUNDNESS_CORRECTNESS.justification: The main claims (that introducing ngrams improves word representations) are supported by experimental results reported across multiple methods and datasets (e.g. "bi-grams bring around 8 percent increase" on Google analogy and "around 10 percent improvements" on MSR). The paper follows established baselines and datasets ("The datasets used in this paper is the same with (Levy et al., 2015)") and enforces fair comparisons (clean strategy and window-size control). However, important experimental details are missing (no statistical significance tests are reported) and the authors admit they did not tune hyper-parameters for the ngram variants ("we strictly follow the hyper-parameters used in baseline models, making no adjustments"), which slightly weakens the conclusiveness of some claims.

ORIGINALITY.justification: The paper applies the fairly natural idea of incorporating ngrams into several existing representation methods (SGNS, GloVe, PPMI, SVD) rather than proposing a fundamentally new modeling paradigm. Related prior uses of phrases/ngrams are acknowledged (e.g. Mikolov et al., 2013b; Li et al., 2016), and the main novelty appears to be a unified treatment across multiple algorithms plus a "novel way of building co-occurrence matrix" to reduce hardware cost. This constitutes a useful engineering/empirical contribution but is incremental rather than highly novel.

CLARITY.justification: The paper is organized into standard sections (related work, model, experiments, conclusion) and defines its SGNS/Ngam formulations formally (e.g. equations (1)-(3) and notation for C(w)). However, the provided text contains repetitions and several typographical/formatting issues and some descriptions are terse; figures and tables are referenced but not shown here, and some implementation choices (e.g. exact hyperparameters, training epochs) are not fully detailed, which reduces readability and reproducibility.

SUBSTANCE.justification: The experimental suite spans multiple embeddings (SGNS, GloVe, PPMI, SVD) and multiple evaluation datasets (six similarity and two analogy datasets) and reports notable gains for certain settings (especially PPMI uni-bi). The authors acknowledge negative results (SVD sometimes worse) and discuss limitations (hyperparameter settings, discarded techniques) and propose a matrix-building strategy to reduce hardware costs. Yet the paper lacks thorough ablation/sensitivity studies (e.g. varying hyper-parameters for ngram models) and statistical analyses needed to fully characterize when and why ngrams help.

IMPACT.justification: The problem—improving word representations by incorporating ngrams—is relevant to NLP and could inform both embedding research and downstream applications. Reported gains on analogy tasks (notably for PPMI) suggest practical value, and the reduced-cost co-occurrence matrix construction could increase accessibility. Nevertheless, the contribution is incremental and unlikely to fundamentally change how researchers think about embeddings without deeper analysis of generalization and downstream task benefits.

APPROPRIATENESS.justification: The topic is squarely within the scope of top NLP/ML conferences (word representation methods and empirical evaluation). The paper uses the standard baselines, datasets, and evaluation protocols expected at such venues (Levy et al., 2015 benchmarks, SGNS/GloVe/PPMI/SVD comparisons) and is aimed at the right audience. Some presentation and analysis gaps may need addressing for a top-tier acceptance, but the work is appropriate for the venue.

MEANINGFUL_COMPARISON.justification: The paper compares the proposed ngram variants against appropriate and recent baselines (SGNS/word2vec, GloVe, PPMI, SVD) using the same datasets as prior work ("same with (Levy et al., 2015)") and enforces fairness via a clean strategy and controlled window sizes. The authors honestly report when baselines outperform their variants (e.g. SVD sometimes worse) and discuss possible causes (hyper-parameter choices), though deeper analysis of differences and inclusion of statistical tests would strengthen the comparison.
